Meta Title: The Otter Guys Reviews  - Plumbing & HVAC
Meta Description: See what our customers say about our reliable HVAC & plumbing services. Read real reviews and discover why Central Virginia trusts The Otter Guys.

H1: The Otter Guys Reviews

Page copy:
At The Otter Guys, we believe the best measure of our work is the satisfaction of the customers we serve. Across Central Virginia, homeowners and businesses trust our team for dependable heating, cooling, indoor air quality, and plumbing services delivered with professionalism and care. Our client reviews reflect the commitment we bring to every project, whether it’s a quick repair, a full system installation, or ongoing maintenance for commercial properties.

Customers often highlight our fast response times, honest recommendations, and knowledgeable technicians. We understand how stressful HVAC or plumbing problems can be, especially when they interrupt your comfort or business operations. That’s why we focus on clear communication, reliable workmanship, and solutions designed to last. From emergency furnace repairs during cold Virginia winters to air conditioning installations that improve summer comfort, we work hard to exceed expectations at every step.

We are proud of the reputation we’ve built through hard work, honesty, and exceptional customer service. Every review motivates us to continue delivering the high-quality heating, cooling, IAQ, and plumbing services Central Virginia residents and businesses deserve.

"Over the past 30 days, 159 people have interacted with your widgets, and 12 of those have reached out via contact form, email, or phone. That’s just part of the value our platform provides. The other half comes from your website widget, powered by Google Maps. You’ve done a great job showcasing your work, which is crucial because Google indexes your website every 30 days—picking up photos, tags, descriptions, and your Google reviews. This creates a synergy that strengthens your brand and boosts your online visibility over time.

To illustrate this, I’ve attached a screenshot showing your mapped jobs and reviews alongside a geo-grid heat map. Each circle represents 5 miles, and the number inside shows your ranking for the keyword “Plumber.”

You can also view your performance over time using this comparison page for the keyword “Plumber” dating from June 2024 to September 2025: View Comparison.

To further illustrate the value, I’ve also attached a screenshot of your recent projects page. When you right-click this page, it’s called the “Inspect Page.” Google checks your account roughly once a month, looking for new activity, content, and the location data—longitude and latitude—of each job. Every job you place on the map represents an area and signals that location to Google. In the attached screenshot, I’ve circled the relevant sections on the inspect page, where you can see the longitude and latitude for each job. This is how Google identifies your work each month, helping improve your visibility and search ranking over time.

I’ll be giving you a call shortly to review next steps and ensure you’re aware of all your options before moving forward with canceling the account and removing the widget. If there’s a time that works best for you, just let me know. I’ll keep the call brief and respectful of your time."

title: PPC Landing Page / Subdomain setup
description:
Hi opening a discussion task for a ticket we got. I am adding the verbatim below to be sure:

Please evaluate the below request for feasibility and time frame for implementation. Let Corey or Ethan know so they can communicate back to Aaron. 

SUMMARY:
The client, The Otter Guys, is requesting the setup of a subdomain for hosting PPC landing pages, referencing a chat from last week. They have provided the HTML and three image assets for the first page ("Sewer Campaign") and have outlined a set of specific, immediate technical requirements for the setup and page deployment.-----Project Management Team Focus

Scope and Deliverables:
  • Core Goal: Set up a dedicated subdomain off the main website (calltheotterguys.com) to host PPC landing pages.
  • Initial Deliverable: Deployment of the first landing page (Sewer Campaign) at the URL /sewer-line-repair/ on the new subdomain.
  • Required Assets:
    • One HTML file (Sewer Campaign landing page).
    • Three image files (SewerPhoto1.jpg, SewerCamera2.jpg, SewerPhoto3.jpg).
  • Key Dependencies/Flags: Requires coordination with the development team for technical setup and configuration steps related to the Google Tag and WP Rocket. The client may handle image paths post-subdomain setup if it is out of scope.
Action Items for PM:
  • Confirm subdomain details and technical feasibility with the Dev Team.
  • Confirm timeline for completion, factoring in the required technical configurations.
  • Communicate back to the client regarding scope, any out-of-scope items (e.g., if they are handling the final image path updates), and the expected delivery date.

-----Development Team Focus
 
Required Technical Tasks:
  1. Subdomain Setup: Configure the infrastructure to host a subdomain off calltheotterguys.com.
  2. Asset Management:
    • Upload the three attached image files (SewerPhoto1.jpg, SewerCamera2.jpg, SewerPhoto3.jpg) to the WordPress Media Library.
    • Update the HTML source paths from the current relative paths to the correct absolute paths referencing the Media Library upload locations.
  3. URL Deployment: Deploy the provided HTML page to the subdomain at the path /sewer-line-repair/.
  4. Tag Integration: Ensure the existing Google Tag (used for CallRail conversion tracking) is applied to calltheotterguys.com is also applied to the new subdomain.
  5. Caching Exclusion: Exclude the new page URL (/sewer-line-repair/) from caching via WP Rocket to ensure CallRail's Dynamic Number Insertion (DNI) functions correctly per-visitor.
  6. Indexing Control: Set the page to noindex in the Yoast plugin, as a "belt-and-suspenders" measure, even though the <meta name="robots" content="noindex"> tag is already hardcoded in the HTML.
Action Items for Dev:
  • Evaluate technical feasibility and estimate time for the subdomain configuration and page deployment tasks.
  • Confirm if the image path updates are fully in-scope for your team.
  • Coordinate with PM on any technical blockers or clarification needed.
 
Thanks much

cc

title: Reporting Call Prep
description:
This task must be fully updated by EOD Monday before the reporting call.

This document serves as:
  • Internal alignment between AM, CM, and SEO
  • Source of truth for call structure
  • Risk detection tool
  • Talking point preparation
No one should walk into the reporting call unprepared.

This prep aligns directly with the live call agenda.

AM should use this document to guide the call in real time.

No one should enter the call without this fully filled out.

SECTION 1: OPENING FRAME (AM)


What tone are we setting?

Client temperature this month:
 ☐ Strong
 ☐ Neutral
 ☐ Slightly concerned
 ☐ Frustrated

Notes from weekly pulse:


Any relationship items to acknowledge at opening?

Opening focus reminder:
What do we want the client to feel by minute 3?

SECTION 2: PERFORMANCE OVERVIEW (CM SECTION)


@LeadSEO please provide by Wednesday 12 PM:
  1. How is performance this month?
  2. Campaign health status: Green / Yellow / Red
  3. Areas of concern
  4. Anything stand out? Wins or things we need to be aware of?
  5. Do you need anything from the client?
  6. What is our main focus next month?
AM must review and confirm alignment with CM before call.

Risk Flag:
 ☐ No performance concerns
 ☐ Minor fluctuation
 ☐ Underperformance risk

If risk exists:
  • Has CM been aligned?
  • Has AM Lead been notified?
No surprises allowed on call.

SECTION 3: PERFORMANCE Q&A PREP


List anticipated performance questions:




Confirm:

☐ CM prepared for these
☐ Messaging aligned
☐ Time boundary plan set

SECTION 4: WORK COMPLETED (AM SECTION)


This mirrors the “Campaign Execution” portion of the call.

List in client-friendly language:
  • SEO deliverables completed:

  • Content published:

  • Links built:

  • Technical fixes:

  • Dev completed:

  • Wins (Sentinel, visibility gains, authority growth):

No internal jargon.

SECTION 5: WORK IN PROGRESS (AM)


  • Active SEO initiatives:

  • Content pipeline:

  • Technical initiatives:

  • Dev in progress:

  • Strategic pushes:

Must clearly show momentum.

SECTION 6: TEAM NEEDS (AM)


Do we need anything from the client?
  • Approvals:

  • Content:

  • Access:

  • Decisions:


If nothing needed, state clearly: None at this time.

SECTION 7: MONTH AHEAD VISION (AM)


This section drives confidence.

Answer clearly:
  • What is our main focus next month?
  • What are we monitoring closely?
  • What would success look like by next reporting call?

SECTION 8: FINAL Q&A PREP (AM)


Non-performance questions the client may raise:


Plan response or follow-up pathway.

Confirm:

☐ If unsure, defer and follow up
☐ CM will not re-enter unless pre-discussed

SECTION 9: ESCALATION CHECK


Before call, confirm:

☐ SEO updates received
☐ Dev updates confirmed
☐ Any blockers escalated
☐ AM Lead notified if risk exists
☐ CM aligned on any sensitive topics

If any box cannot be checked, escalate before call.

SECTION 10: CALL CONTROL REMINDERS (AM)


  • Set agenda at beginning
  • Protect time at minute 20
  • Transition clearly
  • Do not guess
  • Close confidently

title: Include Sitemap In footer of Client websites
description:
Hi, this is inline w/ a group of tasks we have from a sheet. Here is the link for reference: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1qPfUUC1J2r1s7Ot8Y1PmIcje6WpyMszDhxHGz4TxDTk/edit?gid=0#gid=0

SEO added more info column D. 

I have included the task info here:
--- 
Hey Team!

To enhance our technical SEO baseline, improve crawl efficiency, and ensure consistent signaling across all client properties, It would be great to come to an agreement to outlining a new, standardized protocol for crawl management.

My suggestion is as follows:

Implement the following three directives on all client websites as part of our standard build and maintenance process.

1. XML Sitemap in robots.txt (Primary Directive)

This is the most effective way to inform search engines of all indexable URLs.

Action: Ensure the robots.txt file for every client domain contains a Sitemap directive pointing to the full URL of the XML sitemap.

Implementation Example:
User-agent: 
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap.xml

The "Why": This provides a clear, direct path for Google to find and crawl our most important pages, ensuring faster discovery and indexing of new content.

2. User-Facing HTML Sitemap (Footer)

This is for user experience and secondary crawl pathing.

Action: All client websites must have a user-facing HTML sitemap.

Implementation: This sitemap must be linked in the footer of every page on the site.

The "Why": This provides a clear navigation path for users, improves site accessibility, and   acts as a secondary crawl path for search engines to discover deep pages.

Strategic Crawl & Indexation Control

This is critical. We must be precise in telling crawlers what to crawl and what to index.

A. To Control Crawling (Using robots.txt)

We must Disallow crawlers from accessing low-value, non-SEO pages to conserve crawl budget.

Action: Audit and update the robots.txt file to Disallow non-public or utility sections of the site.
Implementation Examples:
Disallow: /wp-admin/

The WP sitemap page  plugin in perfect to use as a plugin for now

Regarding providing a robot.txt:

They differ per client especially regarding disallows but here is a comprehensive one that can be edited according to client needs especially regarding disallows:

User-agent: *

# --- Core WordPress File Disallows ---
# Block all core administrative and include folders.
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Disallow: /wp-includes/

# --- Crawl Budget Optimization ---
# Block non-media plugin and theme files from being crawled.
Disallow: /wp-content/plugins/
Disallow: /wp-content/themes/

# Block internal search result pages, which are low-quality, duplicate content.
Disallow: /?s=
Disallow: /search/

# Block other non-public WordPress files and pages.
Disallow: /wp-login.php
Disallow: /readme.html
Disallow: /trackback/
Disallow: /xmlrpc.php

# --- Allow Directives for Critical Files ---
# Explicitly ALLOW critical AJAX and asset files that Google
# may need for rendering, even if their parent folder is disallowed.
Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php
Allow: /wp-content/uploads/

# --- Sitemap Directive ---
# This is the primary directive to tell all search engines where our XML sitemap is located.
# [IMPORTANT: Replace with the client's actual sitemap URL]
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap_index.xml

Also, just an example of placement. Sitemap listed at the very bottom of the footer on the left followed by privacy policy etc.

* In the footer we will use the page /sitemap/ and not the XML sitemap.  The XML sitemap is for Google & the robots.txt.


you can use this as robots.txt:

# --- Core WordPress File Disallows ---
# Block all core administrative and include folders.
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Disallow: /wp-includes/

# --- Crawl Budget Optimization ---

Disallow: /wp-content/plugins/

# Block internal search result pages, which are low-quality, duplicate content.
Disallow: /?s=

# --- Allow Directives for Critical Files ---

Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php

# --- Sitemap Directive ---
